title: Add the Same Number to Both Sides
description: Use the addition property of equality to create an equivalent equation by adding the same number to both sides.
condition: An equation of the form A = B where a number k is added to both sides.
response: Rewrite as A + k = B + k.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Also used with variable terms, e.g., adding $4x$ to both sides.

title: Apply the Distributive Property
description: Expand or factor expressions using the distributive property of multiplication over addition.
condition: An expression of the form a(b + c) or ab + ac with a common factor.
response: Rewrite as ab + ac (expansion) or a(b + c) (factoring).
label: Principle / Model
notes: Includes both distributing and factoring using the same structural principle.

title: Classify System by Comparing Slopes
description: Determine the number of solutions of a linear system by comparing slopes and intercepts of the two equations.
condition: Two linear equations in slope-intercept form y = mx + b (or rewritten into that form).
response: If slopes are different → one solution; if slopes equal and intercepts different → no solution; if both equal → infinitely many solutions.
label: Principle / Model
example: For $y=2x-4$ and $y=-\tfrac{1}{2}x-4$, slopes 2 and $-\tfrac{1}{2}$ differ, so there is exactly one solution.

title: Classify System with Same Slope, Different Intercepts
description: Determine that a system of two linear equations has no solution when the lines have equal slopes but different y-intercepts.
condition: Two linear equations in slope-intercept form with equal slopes and different y-intercepts.
response: State that the system has no solution.
label: Principle / Model
example: Given $y = 3x + 5$ and $y = 3x + 3$, both have slope $3$ but different y-intercepts, so the system has no solution.
notes: Relies on understanding that parallel distinct lines do not intersect.

title: Combine Like Terms in Linear Expression
description: Combine terms with the same variable or constants to simplify a linear expression.
condition: An algebraic expression contains like terms (e.g., ax + bx or c + d).
response: Add or subtract coefficients of like terms to produce a simplified expression.
label: Principle / Model
example: Simplify $180t - 540 + 100$ by combining constants: $-540 + 100 = -440$, so the result is $180t - 440$.

title: Compare Coefficients to Determine Number of Solutions
description: Determine whether a linear equation has one solution by comparing coefficients of the variable on both sides.
condition: A linear equation in one variable has different coefficients of the variable on each side (e.g., ax + b = cx + d with a ≠ c).
response: Conclude that the equation has exactly one solution.
label: Principle / Model
example: In $3x + 7 = 5x + 7$, since $3 \ne 5$, the equation has one solution.

title: Determine if Equations Are Equivalent
description: Determine whether two equations are equivalent by comparing their solution sets.
condition: Two equations in the same variable are given.
response: Decide whether they have the same solution set (state Yes or No with justification).
label: Principle / Model
example: Compare $4x + 2 = 20x$ and $x + 2 = 5x$. Solving the second gives $x = \tfrac{1}{2}$, which does not satisfy the first, so they are not equivalent.
notes: Justification may involve solving both equations or testing a solution.

title: Divide Both Sides by the Same Nonzero Number
description: Use the division property of equality to isolate a variable by dividing both sides by the same nonzero coefficient.
condition: An equation of the form aA = aB where a ≠ 0.
response: Rewrite as A = B by dividing both sides by a.
label: Principle / Model

title: Form Total Count Equation
description: Translate a context with a fixed total number of events into an equation summing category counts.
condition: Context gives total number of events and variables represent category counts.
response: Write an equation of the form $x + y = \text{total events}$.
label: Rule / Plan
example: If there are 5 total rounds, write $x + y = 5$.

title: Identify Structure for Infinitely Many Solutions
description: Recognize that a linear equation has infinitely many solutions when both sides are equivalent expressions.
condition: A linear equation simplifies to identical expressions on both sides (e.g., ax + b = ax + b).
response: Conclude the equation has infinitely many solutions.
label: Principle / Model
example: The equation $2x + 4 = 2x + 4$ is true for all x, so it has infinitely many solutions.

title: Interpret Equation Solution in Context
description: Explain the meaning of a solution to an equation within the context of a real-world situation.
condition: A value that solves a contextual equation is found.
response: State what the value represents in terms of the situation described.
label: Rule / Plan
example: If $t = 22$ solves $160t = 180(t - 3) + 100$, then 22 minutes after Friend A starts, both friends are the same distance from the starting line.

title: Interpret Graph Intersection as Solution
description: Understand that the intersection point of two lines represents the solution to the system.
response: Identify ordered pair where the lines intersect as the solution.
label: Concept
example: If lines intersect at $(3,55)$, then the solution to the system is $(3,55)$.

title: Interpret Parallel Lines Graphically
description: Describe the graph of a system with no solutions as parallel lines.
condition: System of linear equations determined to have no solutions.
response: State that the graphs are parallel lines.
label: Principle / Model
example: If a system has no solution because slopes are equal and intercepts differ, the lines are parallel.

title: Model Linear Relationship from Context
description: Translate a verbal situation with constant rate of change into a linear equation.
condition: Context describing initial value and constant rate (increase or decrease).
response: Write equation in form $y = mx + b$ with slope as rate and intercept as initial value.
label: Principle / Model
example: Han starts with $100$ and spends $15$ per week: $H = 100 - 15x$.

title: Recognize Division Must Apply to All Terms
description: When dividing both sides of an equation by a number, each term on that side must be divided.
condition: An equation where one side has multiple terms and a division by a common factor is performed.
response: Divide every term on that side by the factor; recognize errors if a term is omitted.
label: Principle / Model
example: From $4x + 2 = 20x$, dividing the left side by 4 correctly gives $x + \tfrac{1}{2}$, not $x + 2$.
notes: Supports reasoning about why two equations are not equivalent.

title: Set Up System from Contextual Constraints
description: Form a system of equations representing totals and constraints in a word problem.
condition: Word problem describing totals (e.g., total players or total games) and per-unit quantities.
response: Write equations representing each constraint using variables for unknown quantities.
label: Principle / Model
example: If $x$ is 6-player games and $y$ is 2-player games: $6x+2y=50$ and $x+y=13$.

title: Solve System by Substitution
description: Substitute an expression for one variable into the other equation to solve a system.
condition: A system where one equation is solved for a variable (e.g., $y = ...$).
response: Replace that variable in the other equation and solve resulting one-variable equation.
label: Principle / Model

title: Substitute to Verify System Solution
description: Check whether an ordered pair is a solution to a system by substituting into both equations.
condition: Given a system of equations and a candidate ordered pair $(x, y)$.
response: Substitute the values into both equations and verify each equation is true.
label: Rule / Plan
example: Check $(7,10)$ in $3x+5y=19$ and $x+y=5$ by evaluating both sides and confirming equality.
notes: Defines solution as making both equations true simultaneously.

title: Subtract Same Term from Both Sides
description: Maintain equality by subtracting the same expression from both sides of an equation.
condition: An equation contains the same variable term on both sides (e.g., ax + b = cx + d).
response: Subtract one variable term (e.g., cx) from both sides to collect like terms.
label: Principle / Model
example: From $160t = 180t - 440$, subtract $180t$ from both sides to get $-20t = -440$.
